sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous represent a comprehensive approach to the diagnosis, staging, and treatment of cutaneous melanoma. The treatment philosophy is multimodal, integrating surgical, systemic, and radiation therapies depending on disease stage and risk. For localized disease, surgical excision remains the primary curative treatment with defined margin recommendations based on Breslow thickness. Systemic adjuvant therapy with immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1) or BRAF/MEK targeted therapy (for BRAF-mutant tumors) is considered for resected stage IIB/IIC through stage IV disease. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy has emerged as a preferred approach for clinically positive nodal disease, with the NADINA and SWOG S1801 trials demonstrating improved event-free survival with neoadjuvant regimens compared to adjuvant-only approaches [1], [2]. For unresectable or metastatic disease, immune checkpoint inhibitors including anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) or combination ipilimumab/nivolumab represent preferred first-line options, with BRAF/MEK targeted therapy as an alternative for patients with BRAF V600-mutant tumors [3], [4]. Treatment decisions should be individualized based on patient performance status, comorbidities, tumor burden, BRAF mutation status, and patient preferences through shared decision-making.

nodes.label: Surgical excision with margins based on Breslow thickness
Assess SLNB risk: not recommended for T1a <0.8 mm without ulceration; considered for T1b or T1a with adverse features; recommended for stage IB (T2a) or stage II (T2b+) where risk >10%
SLNB discussed and offered based on risk; observation for low risk
Neoadjuvant systemic therapy preferred (ipilimumab/nivolumab or pembrolizumab) for clinically positive nodes
Adjuvant systemic therapy for resected high-risk (stage IIB/IIC through IV) or recurrent disease: nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+, duration 12 months
Unresectable or metastatic disease: assess BRAF mutation status and performance status

First-line preferred: combination checkpoint (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab) – all category 1; BRAF/MEK inhibitors alternative for BRAF V600-mutant
Consider MDT consultation for borderline resectable, brain mets, desmoplastic melanoma, locally advanced/metastatic planning

edges.label: based on Breslow thickness and ulceration

cards.title: Curative - Localized Disease
cards.detail: Surgical excision with defined margins based on Breslow thickness is the primary treatment. SLNB is discussed and offered based on risk of nodal positivity: not recommended for T1a melanomas <0.8 mm without ulceration (SLN positivity <5%), considered for T1b melanomas or T1a with adverse features (5-10% risk), and recommended for stage IB (T2a) or stage II (T2b+) where risk is >10% [5], [6].

cards.title: Adjuvant - Resected High-Risk Disease
cards.detail: Adjuvant systemic therapy options include nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab (category 1 for clinically positive nodes), or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600 mutation positive. The choice between anti-PD-1 monotherapy and observation involves shared decision-making weighing recurrence risk against treatment toxicity, including approximately 15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction [7], [8]. Duration is 12 months for all regimens.

cards.title: Neoadjuvant - Resectable Advanced Disease
cards.detail: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for clinically positive nodes. Preferred regimens include ipilimumab/nivolumab (category 1 for clinically positive nodes without prior therapy) or pembrolizumab. Neoadjuvant approaches show improved EFS compared to adjuvant-only strategies (12-month EFS 84% vs 57% for nivolumab/ipilimumab in NADINA; 2-year EFS 72% vs 49% for pembrolizumab in SWOG 1801) [1], [2].

cards.title: Palliative/Therapeutic - Unresectable/Metastatic Disease
cards.detail: First-line preferred options include combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab or nivolumab), all category 1. BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations are category 1 alternatives for BRAF V600-mutant tumors. Combination checkpoint therapy is preferred over monotherapy for patients with good performance status who can tolerate higher toxicity rates (grade 3-5: 54-59% for combination vs 22% for anti-PD-1) [3], [4], [9].
